takeaway: The American Marketing Association's official definition: marketing is "the activity, set of institutions, and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, clients, partners, and society at large" — framing marketing as value exchange, not just advertising.

Santa Clara University frames the 4 Ps (product, price, place, promotion) as an organized structure for strategy, noting "In the 1960s, E. Jerome McCarthy introduced the 4 Ps classification, refining the earlier 'marketing mix' concept proposed by Neil Borden."

A customer value proposition states why a customer should buy — "the sum total of benefits which a vendor promises a customer will receive in return for the customer's associated payment" — positioned as solving the customer's problem better than alternatives.

The STP framework chains three steps: segment the market into distinct groups, target the most profitable ones, then position for each — "Positioning proposes distinctive competitive advantages for each segment."

Market segmentation is "the process of dividing mass markets into groups with similar needs and wants"; the four common bases are demographic (age, income), geographic (location), psychographic (lifestyle, values), and behavioral (usage, purchase habits).

Targeting picks which segments to serve via four strategies — undifferentiated (one message for all), differentiated (custom messages per group), concentrated (one niche), and micromarketing — noting "Digital communications have allowed marketers to segment markets at ever tighter levels - right down to the individual consumer."

Positioning is the perception a brand holds in the consumer's mind, popularized by Ries and Trout as "an organized system for finding a window in the mind"; marketers use perceptual maps to plot brands on axes like price and quality to find gaps.

Product Marketing Alliance frames the 4 Ps as an integrated system: "These four elements—product, price, place, and promotion—form the foundation of any successful marketing strategy and help you view your offering through your customer's eyes."

The marketing mix is "the set of marketing tools that the firm uses to pursue its marketing objectives in the target market"; McCarthy's 4 Ps were later extended to the 7 Ps (adding people, process, physical evidence) for services and reframed as Lauterborn's customer-centric 4 Cs.

HubSpot traces AIDA (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action) to Elias St. Elmo Lewis in 1898, noting "AIDA is more linear. It focuses on psychological progression rather than just customer journey touchpoints" — the ancestor of the marketing funnel.

CFI describes AIDA as identifying "the stages that an individual goes through during the process of purchasing a product or service," with modern variants (AIDCAS, REAN) adding confidence, satisfaction, and nurturing stages the original omits.

MindTools applies AIDA to copywriting: "These are the four steps you need to take your audience through if you want them to buy your product or visit your website" — a practical lens on moving a reader from notice to action.

The purchase funnel depicts the journey from awareness to purchase; it grew out of Lewis's AIDA and was joined to the funnel shape by William Townsend in 1924 — "The process is continually from the general to the specific... to lead a customer from Attention to Interest, and beyond."

Kotler's 5 A's customer path (Aware, Appeal, Ask, Act, Advocate) updates the linear funnel for a connected world; the map "can help them design practices that make the customer's purchasing process smoother and more satisfying," with advocacy looping back to awareness.

The product life cycle runs through introduction, growth, maturity, and decline — "Product sales pass through distinct stages, each posing different challenges, opportunities, and problems to the seller" — so the right marketing mix shifts stage by stage.
